questionText:
If the value of real number $a>0$ for which $x^2-5 a x+1=0$ and $x^2-a x-5=0$

have a common real root is $\frac{3}{\sqrt{2 \beta}}$ then $\beta$ is equal to ___________.
subject: Mathematics
solution:
$${x^2} - 5\alpha x + 1 = 0$$ ..... (1)

$${x^2} - \alpha x - 5 = 0$$ ...... (2)

have a common root.

Subtracting (1) with (2) we'll get $$x = {6 \over {4\alpha }}$$

Substituting in (1)

$${{36} \over {16{\alpha ^2}}} - {{30} \over 4} + 1 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\alpha ^2} = {9 \over {26}}$$

$$\alpha = {3 \over {\sqrt {2 \times 13} }}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\beta = 13$$

questionText: Let a, b $$\in$$ R be such that the equation $$a{x^2} - 2bx + 15 = 0$$ has a repeated root $$\alpha$$. If $$\alpha$$ and $$\beta$$ are the roots of the equation $${x^2} - 2bx + 21 = 0$$, then $${\alpha ^2} + {\beta ^2}$$ is equal to :
subject: Mathematics
solution:
$$a{x^2} - 2bx + 15 = 0$$ has repeated root so $${b^2} = 15a$$ and $$\alpha = {{15} \over b}$$

$$\because$$ $$\alpha$$ is a root of $${x^2} - 2bx + 21 = 0$$

So $${{225} \over {{b^2}}} = 9 \Rightarrow {b^2} = 25$$

Now $${\alpha ^2} + {\beta ^2} = {(\alpha + \beta )^2} - 2\alpha \beta = 4{b^2} - 42 = 100 - 42 = 58$$

questionText: If for some $$\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{q}, \mathrm{r} \in \mathbf{R}$$, not all have same sign, one of the roots of the equation $$\left(\mathrm{p}^{2}+\mathrm{q}^{2}\right) x^{2}-2 \mathrm{q}(\mathrm{p}+\mathrm{r}) x+\mathrm{q}^{2}+\mathrm{r}^{2}=0$$ is also a root of the equation $$x^{2}+2 x-8=0$$, then $$\frac{\mathrm{q}^{2}+\mathrm{r}^{2}}{\mathrm{p}^{2}}$$ is equal to ____________,
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let roots of

$$\therefore$$ $$\alpha$$ + $$\beta$$ > 0 and $$\alpha$$$$\beta$$ > 0

Also, it has a common root with $${x^2} + 2x - 8 = 0$$

$$\therefore$$ The common root between above two equations is 4.

$$ \Rightarrow 16({p^2} + {q^2}) - 8q(p + r) + {q^2} + {r^2} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (16{p^2} - 8pq + {q^2}) + (16{q^2} - 8qr + {r^2}) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(4p - q)^2} + {(4q - r)^2} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow q = 4p$$ and $$r = 16p$$

$$\therefore$$ $${{{q^2} + {r^2}} \over {{p^2}}} = {{16{p^2} + 256{p^2}} \over {{p^2}}} = 272$$

questionText: Let $$\lambda$$ $$\ne$$ 0 be in R. If $$\alpha$$ and $$\beta$$ are the roots of the equation x<sup>2</sup> $$-$$ x + 2$$\lambda$$ = 0, and $$\alpha$$ and $$\gamma$$ are the roots of equation 3x<sup>2</sup> $$-$$ 10x + 27$$\lambda$$ = 0, then $${{\beta \gamma } \over \lambda }$$ is equal to ____________.
subject: Mathematics
solution:
3$$\alpha$$2 $$-$$ 10$$\alpha$$ + 27$$\lambda$$ = 0 ..... (1)

$$\alpha$$2 $$-$$ $$\alpha$$ + 2$$\lambda$$ = 0 ...... (2)

(1) $$-$$ 3(2) gives

$$-$$7$$\alpha$$ + 21$$\lambda$$ = 0 $$\Rightarrow$$ $$\alpha$$ = 3$$\lambda$$

Put $$\alpha$$ = 3$$\lambda$$ in equation (1) we get

9$$\lambda$$2 $$-$$ 3$$\lambda$$ + 2$$\lambda$$ $$-$$ 0

9$$\lambda$$2 = $$\lambda$$ $$\Rightarrow$$ $$\lambda$$ = $${1 \over 9}$$ as $$\lambda$$ $$\ne$$ 0

Now, $$\alpha$$ = 3$$\lambda$$ $$\Rightarrow$$ $$\lambda$$ = $${1 \over 3}$$

$$\alpha$$ + $$\beta$$ = 1 $$\Rightarrow$$ $$\beta$$ = 2/3

$$\alpha$$ + $$\gamma$$ = $${10 \over 3}$$ $$\Rightarrow$$ $$\gamma$$ = 3

$${{\beta \gamma } \over \lambda } = {{{2 \over 3} \times 3} \over {{1 \over 9}}} = 18$$

questionText:
Let a, b $$ \in $$ R, a $$ \ne $$ 0 be such that the equation,
ax<sup>2</sup> – 2bx + 5 = 0 has a repeated root $$\alpha $$, which
is also a root of the equation, x<sup>2</sup> – 2bx – 10 = 0.
If $$\beta $$ is the other root of this equation, then
$$\alpha $$<sup>2</sup> + $$\beta $$<sup>2</sup> is equal to :
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Roots of equation ax2 – 2bx + 5 = 0 are $$\alpha $$, $$\alpha $$.

$$ \therefore $$ $$\alpha $$ + $$\alpha $$ = $${{2b} \over a}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 2$$\alpha $$ = $${{2b} \over a}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$\alpha $$ = $${{b} \over a}$$ ....(1)

and $$\alpha $$2 = $${5 \over a}$$ ....(2)

From (1) and (2), we get

$${{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}} = {5 \over a}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ b2 = 5a

$$\alpha $$, $$\beta $$ are the roots of equation x2 – 2bx – 10 = 0

$$ \therefore $$ $$\alpha $$ + $$\beta $$ = 2b

and $$\alpha $$$$\beta $$ = -10

As $$\alpha $$ is a root of the equation x2 – 2bx – 10 = 0.

$$ \therefore $$ $$\alpha $$2 - 2b$$\alpha $$ - 10 = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}} - {{2{b^2}} \over a} - 10 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${{5a} \over {{a^2}}} - {{10a} \over a} - 10 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${5 \over a} - 10 - 10 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$a$$ = $${1 \over 4}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${\alpha ^2}$$ = 20

$$\alpha $$$$\beta $$ = -10

$$ \Rightarrow $$$${\beta ^2}$$ = 5

$$ \therefore $$ $${\alpha ^2} + {\beta ^2}$$ = 20 + 5 = 25

questionText:
If $$\alpha $$, $$\beta $$ and $$\gamma $$ are three consecutive terms of a non-constant G.P. such that the equations $$\alpha $$x
<sup>2</sup>
 + 2$$\beta $$x + $$\gamma $$ = 0 and
x<sup>2</sup>
 + x – 1 = 0 have a common root, then $$\alpha $$($$\beta $$ + $$\gamma $$) is equal to :
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let the common ratio of G.P is r

Then the equation of $$\alpha {x^2} + 2\beta x + \gamma = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$\alpha {x^2} + 2\alpha rx + \alpha {r^2} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ x2 + 2rx + r2 = 0 ........(i)
Equation (i) and x2 + x - 1 = 0 ......... (ii) has a common root.

Now (i) - (ii) $$ \Rightarrow $$ (2r-1)x + (r2+1) = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ x = $${{ - \left( {{r^2} + 1} \right)} \over {2r - 1}}$$ ....... (iii)

Now putting (iii) in equation (ii)

$$ \Rightarrow $$ (r2+1)2 - (r2+1) (2r - 1) - (2r - 1)2 = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ r4 - 2r3 - r2 + 2r + 1 = 0 .......... (iv)

dividing equation (iv) by r2

$$ \Rightarrow $$ r2 - 2r - 1 + $${2 \over r} + {1 \over {{r^2}}} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${\left( {r - {1 \over r}} \right)^2} - 2\left( {r - {1 \over r}} \right) + 1 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${\left( {r - {1 \over r} - r} \right)^2} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${{{r^2} - 1} \over r} = 1$$ $$ \Rightarrow $$ r2 = 1 + r

Now $$\alpha $$($$\beta $$ + $$\gamma $$) $$ \Rightarrow $$ $$\alpha $$($$\alpha $$r + $$\alpha $$r2)

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$\alpha ^2 r (1 + r)$$   [$$ \because $$ r2 = 1 + r]

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$\alpha ^2 r . r^2$$ $$ \Rightarrow $$ $$( \alpha r )\,( \alpha r^2 )$$ $$ \Rightarrow $$ $$\beta $$$$\gamma $$

questionText: If the equations x<sup>2</sup> + bx−1 = 0 and x<sup>2</sup> + x + b = 0 have a common root different from −1, then $$\left| b \right|$$ is equal to :
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given,

x2 + bx $$-$$ 1 = 0 . . . . .(1)

and x2 + x + b = 0 . . . . . (2)

Performing (1) $$-$$ (2) we get,

bx $$-$$ 1 $$-$$ x $$-$$ b = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$    x(b $$-$$ 1) = b + 1

$$ \Rightarrow $$   x = $${{b + 1} \over {b - 1}}$$

putting value of x in equation (2),

$${\left( {{{b + 1} \over {b - 1}}} \right)^2} + \left( {{{b + 1} \over {b - 1}}} \right) + b = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$   (b + 1)2 + (b + 1) (b $$-$$ 1) + b (b $$-$$ 1)2 = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$    b2 + 2b + 1 + b2 $$-$$ 1 + b (b2 $$-$$ 2b + 1) = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$    2b3 + 2b + b3 $$-$$ 2b2 + b = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$     b3 + 3b = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$    b(b2 + 3) = 0

b2 = $$-$$ 3, b = 0

$$ \therefore $$    b = $$ \pm \sqrt 3 i$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$    $$\left| b \right|$$ = $$\sqrt 3 $$

questionText: If the equations $${x^2} + 2x + 3 = 0$$ and $$a{x^2} + bx + c = 0,$$ $$a,\,b,\,c\, \in \,R,$$  have a common root, then $$a\,:b\,:c\,$$ is
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given equations are

$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{x^2} + 2x + 3 = 0\,\,\,\,\,...\left( i \right)$$

$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,a{x^2} + bx + c = 0\,\,\,...\left( {ii} \right)$$

Roots of equation $$(i)$$ are imaginary roots.

According to the question $$(ii)$$ will also have both roots same as $$(i).$$

Thus $${a \over 1} = {b \over 2} = {c \over 3} = \lambda \left( {say} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow a = \lambda ,b = 2\lambda ,c = 3\lambda $$

Hence, required ratio is $$1:2:3$$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
The given equations are

$${x^2} + bx - 1 = 0$$

$${x^2} + x + b = 0$$ ....... (1)

Common root is $$(b - 1)x - 1 - b = 0$$.

$$ \Rightarrow x = {{b + 1} \over {b - 1}}$$

This value of x satisfies Eq. (1), we get

$${{{{(b + 1)}^2}} \over {{{(b - 1)}^2}}} + {{b + 1} \over {b - 1}} + b = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow b = i\sqrt 3 ,\, - i\sqrt 3 ,\,0$$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
We have,

$${(2x)^{\ln 2}} = {(3y)^{\ln 3}}$$ ...... (1)

$${3^{\ln x}} = {2^{\ln y}}$$ ....... (2)

$$ \Rightarrow (\log x)(\log 3) = (\log y)\log 2$$

$$ \Rightarrow \log y = {{(\log x)(\log 3)} \over {\log 2}}$$ ........ (3)

Taking log both sides of Eq. (1), we get

$$(\log 2)\{ \log 2 + \log x\} = \log 3\{ \log 3 + \log y\} $$

$${(\log 2)^2} + (\log 2)(\log x) = {(\log 3)^2} + {{{{(\log 3)}^2}(\log x)} \over {\log 2}}$$ from Eq. (3)

$$ \Rightarrow {(\log 2)^2} - {(\log 3)^2} = {{{{(\log 3)}^2} - {{(\log 2)}^2}} \over {\log 2}}(\log x)$$

$$ \Rightarrow - \log 2 = \log x$$

$$ \Rightarrow x = {1 \over 2} \Rightarrow {x_0} = {1 \over 2}$$.

questionText: The quadratic equations $${x^2} - 6x + a = 0$$ and $${x^2} - cx + 6 = 0$$ have one root in common. The other roots of the first and second equations are integers in the ratio 4 : 3. Then the common root is
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let the roots of equation $${x^2} - 6x + a = 0$$ be $$\alpha $$

and $$4$$ $$\beta $$ and that of the equation

$${x^2} - cx + 6 = 0$$ be $$\alpha $$ and $$3\beta .$$ Then

$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\alpha + 4\beta = 6;\,\,\,\,\,\,\,4\alpha \beta = a$$

and $$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\alpha + 3\beta = c;\,\,\,\,\,\,\,3\alpha \beta = 6$$

$$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,a = 8$$

$$\therefore$$ The equation becomes

$${x^2} - 6x + 8 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \left( {x - 2} \right)\left( {x - 4} \right) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ roots are $$2$$ and $$4$$

$$ \Rightarrow \alpha = 2,\beta = 1$$

$$\therefore$$ Common root is $$2.$$

questionText:
If one root of the equation $${x^2} + px + 12 = 0$$ is 4, while the equation $${x^2} + px + q = 0$$ has equal roots,
then the value of $$'q'$$ is
subject: Mathematics
solution:
$$4$$ is a root of $${x^2} + px + 12 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 16 + 4p + 12 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow p = - 7$$

Now, the equation $${x^2} + px + q = 0$$

has equal roots.

$$\therefore$$ $${p^2} - 4q = 0$$ $$ \Rightarrow q = {{{p^2}} \over 4} = {{49} \over 4}$$
